name: Mandrake Root
description: The Mandrake Root (Mandragora officinarum) holds a prominent place in European folklore and magic. Its human-like root shape contributed to the belief that it possessed human-like qualities, including the ability to scream when uprooted, a scream said to be fatal to anyone who heard it. To avoid this deadly scream, a dog was often used to pull the root from the ground. The Mandrake was associated with witchcraft, fertility, and protection. It was believed to enhance fertility, bring good fortune, and ward off evil spirits. The plant was considered a powerful ingredient in potions and spells, used for healing, divination, and love magic. Its association with the underworld and its potent properties made it a highly sought-after and feared herb. In some traditions, the Mandrake was also linked to the gallows, as it was believed to grow beneath them, nourished by the fluids of hanged criminals, further enhancing its dark reputation. The plant's rarity and difficulty in obtaining it only added to its mystique and value. Its dual nature, capable of both healing and harm, made it a symbol of the complex relationship between humanity and the natural world.
subtitle: Screaming root of magic and mystery

keyMyths.title: The Fatal Scream
keyMyths.description: The Mandrake root screams when pulled from the earth, a sound that can kill anyone who hears it. To avoid this, people used dogs to uproot the plant, sacrificing the animal instead of themselves.

keyMyths.title: Gallows Plant
keyMyths.description: Mandrake roots were believed to grow beneath the gallows, nourished by the fluids of hanged men. This association further enhanced the plant's magical and dangerous reputation.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Discusses the properties and uses of Mandrake in ancient Roman times.
sources.text: Discusses the properties and uses of Mandrake in ancient Roman times.

sources.author: Nicholas Culpeper
sources.description: Details the astrological associations and medicinal uses of Mandrake in herbal medicine.
